topic.title: Standard 4: Equality and Diversity
topic.summary: Understanding equality, diversity and inclusion, recognising discrimination, and knowing how to promote inclusive practice in health and social care.

questions.source_criterion: 4.1a Explain the key terminology: equality, diversity, inclusion, and discrimination
questions.prompt: What does 'diversity' mean in a health and social care context?

options.label: Recognising and valuing that every person is different and unique
Treating everyone exactly the same regardless of their needs
Only focusing on cultural and ethnic differences
Ignoring differences to avoid causing offence

questions.explanation: Diversity is about recognising and valuing that every person is different and unique. It means understanding that each individual has their own background, beliefs, needs and preferences, and that these differences should be respected.

questions.source_criterion: 4.1a Explain the key terminology: equality, diversity, inclusion, and discrimination

options.label: Ensuring everyone has the opportunity to take part in activities and be involved in decisions about their care
Keeping people separate based on their needs
Only including people who can communicate in English
Making decisions for people to save them the trouble

questions.explanation: Inclusion means making sure everyone feels valued and has the opportunity to participate fully, regardless of their background, ability, or circumstances. It is about removing barriers and actively involving people in decisions about their own care.

questions.source_criterion: 4.1b Describe how discrimination can happen deliberately or by mistake
questions.prompt: Which of the following is an example of unintentional discrimination?

options.label: A care home serving only British food because they have not considered the diverse dietary needs of residents
A worker deliberately refusing to help someone because of their religion
Physically assaulting someone because of their disability
Using racist language towards an individual

questions.explanation: Unintentional discrimination happens when a person or organisation does not mean to discriminate, but their actions or policies still create barriers. For example, assuming everyone eats the same food, or scheduling activities that exclude certain people, without realising the impact.

questions.source_criterion: 4.1c Describe how to reduce the likelihood of discrimination
questions.prompt: How can you reduce the likelihood of discrimination happening in a care setting?

options.label: By working in a person-centred way, valuing diversity, and challenging discrimination when you see it
By treating everyone exactly the same regardless of their individual needs
By avoiding any discussion of differences between people
By only following policies and never using your own judgement

questions.explanation: Person-centred working, actively valuing diversity, and challenging discrimination are key to reducing it. You should also be aware of your own attitudes and beliefs, follow your employer's equality policies, and speak up if you witness discrimination.

questions.source_criterion: 4.2a Identify legislation and codes of practice relating to equality and diversity
questions.prompt: Which key piece of legislation protects people from discrimination in the UK?

options.label: The Equality Act 2010
The Health and Safety at Work Act 1974
The Data Protection Act 2018
The Mental Capacity Act 2005

questions.explanation: The Equality Act 2010 legally protects people from discrimination in the workplace and in wider society. It sets out nine protected characteristics: age, disability, gender reassignment, marriage and civil partnership, pregnancy and maternity, race, religion or belief, sex, and sexual orientation.

questions.source_criterion: 4.2a Identify legislation and codes of practice relating to equality and diversity
questions.prompt: Under the Equality Act 2010, which of the following is a 'protected characteristic'?

options.label: Disability
Job title
Length of employment
Salary level

questions.explanation: Disability is one of the nine protected characteristics under the Equality Act 2010. Others include age, gender reassignment, marriage and civil partnership, pregnancy and maternity, race, religion or belief, sex, and sexual orientation.

questions.prompt: Which of the following is an example of direct discrimination?

options.label: A care worker refuses to support someone because of their sexual orientation
A building has no ramp, making it difficult for wheelchair users to enter
Information is only provided in English in a multilingual community
Activities are only scheduled during daytime hours

questions.explanation: Direct discrimination happens when someone is treated less favourably because of a protected characteristic. The other examples are forms of indirect discrimination or lack of reasonable adjustment, where policies or environments create barriers without deliberately targeting anyone.

questions.source_criterion: 4.3a Identify sources of information about equality and diversity
questions.prompt: Where can you find information about your organisation's approach to equality and diversity?

options.label: Your employer's equality and diversity policy, your manager, and your induction training
Equality and diversity information is confidential and not shared with staff
Only from external organisations like the CQC
You do not need this information — it is common sense

questions.explanation: Your employer should have an equality and diversity policy that you can access. You will also learn about it during your induction, and your manager can provide guidance. External sources include the Care Quality Commission (CQC) and the Equality and Human Rights Commission.

questions.source_criterion: 4.3b and c Identify when to get additional information about equality and diversity
questions.prompt: When might you need to seek additional information or support about equality and diversity?

options.label: When you are unsure how to meet an individual's specific needs related to their background or beliefs
Equality and diversity issues only need to be considered during induction training
You should never need additional support because all policies cover everything
Only when a complaint has been made

questions.explanation: You may need additional information when you are unsure about how to meet someone's specific needs — for example, dietary requirements related to religion, or communication needs related to disability. Your manager and your organisation's policies are good starting points.

questions.prompt: What does 'equality' mean in health and social care?

options.label: Everyone having the same opportunities and being treated fairly according to their individual needs
Treating everyone in exactly the same way all the time
Only giving opportunities to people who can pay for them
Making sure nobody gets any extra help or support

questions.explanation: Equality means everyone has the same opportunities and is treated fairly, but fairness does not always mean treating everyone the same — it means giving each person what they need to have the same opportunities. This is different from treating everyone identically.

questions.prompt: An individual who uses a wheelchair cannot access a social event because it is held in a room upstairs with no lift. What type of discrimination is this?

options.label: Indirect discrimination — the policy or environment creates a barrier without meaning to
Direct discrimination — someone deliberately excluded them
Harassment
Victimisation

questions.explanation: This is indirect discrimination. The organisers may not have intended to exclude wheelchair users, but the lack of a lift creates a barrier that prevents equal access. Under the Equality Act, employers and service providers must make 'reasonable adjustments' to avoid this.

questions.prompt: A care worker always addresses the family member of an individual with dementia rather than speaking directly to the individual themselves. What might this be an example of?

options.label: Discrimination based on disability — making assumptions about what the individual can understand
Good practice — families should always be involved
A time-saving technique that is perfectly acceptable
Nothing wrong — the worker is being helpful

questions.explanation: Assuming someone cannot understand or participate because of their condition is a form of discrimination. Everyone should be treated as an individual and involved in their own care as much as possible, regardless of any disability or condition.

questions.prompt: What is the best way to respond if you witness a colleague making a discriminatory comment about an individual?

options.label: Calmly challenge the comment and report it to your manager following your organisation's policy
Ignore it — it is not your responsibility to challenge colleagues
Join in to avoid conflict with your colleague
Only report it if the individual complains

questions.explanation: You have a duty to challenge discrimination and report it. Ignoring it or joining in makes the problem worse. Following your organisation's policy and reporting to your manager ensures the issue is properly addressed.
